questions.question: What makes a Temporal Durable Timer different from a simple in-memory sleep or delay in your application code?

choices.text: Durable Timers block the Worker thread so no other tasks can run while waiting.
Durable Timers are persisted by Temporal, so the Workflow resumes correctly even if the Worker or service restarts during the wait.
Durable Timers require a dedicated Worker process that does nothing but track time.
Durable Timers are limited to a maximum wait of 24 hours before they must be renewed.

questions.explanation: Unlike an in-memory sleep that is lost if the process crashes, a Temporal Durable Timer is persisted in the Temporal Service. When the timer expires, the Workflow picks up exactly where it left off — even if the Worker or Temporal Service was down in the meantime. This is the core value of durable execution: your business logic survives infrastructure failures without any extra code.

questions.question: How resource-intensive is it to run a large number of Durable Timers in Temporal?

choices.text: Each Timer needs its own dedicated thread or goroutine, so hundreds of Timers will noticeably strain a single Worker.
Timers consume significant memory because the Workflow's full state must be kept in RAM throughout the wait.
Sleeping is a resource-light operation — millions of Timers can run off a single Worker without tying up the process.
Timers are lightweight only up to a 1-hour duration; longer waits require dedicated infrastructure.

questions.explanation: Because Timers are managed by the Temporal Service rather than held in process memory, sleeping is extremely lightweight. A single Worker can handle millions of concurrent Timers. This is very different from, say, spinning up a thread per sleep — Temporal offloads the waiting to its durable state store.

questions.question: How long can a Temporal Workflow sleep using a Durable Timer?

choices.text: Up to a few minutes, after which the timer must be manually re-triggered by the developer.
Up to 24 hours, which is the maximum supported by the Temporal Service.
Up to one week, matching the default Workflow execution timeout.
For days, months, or even years — there is no enforced short upper limit on timer duration.

questions.explanation: Temporal Durable Timers can span very long periods — days, months, or years. This is a key differentiator from traditional job schedulers or cron systems. Because the timer state is persisted, the Workflow simply waits as long as needed without any polling or keep-alive mechanism on the application side.

questions.question: Why must you use a Workflow-specific sleep function (like `Workflow.sleep` or `Workflow.DelayAsync`) inside a Workflow, rather than the standard language sleep function?

choices.text: Standard sleep functions run faster and would cause the Workflow to skip steps if used directly.
The Workflow-specific function is recorded in Temporal's event history, making the pause durable and replayable, while a standard sleep would break determinism.
Standard sleep functions are not available inside Workflow code due to SDK restrictions on imported libraries.
The Workflow-specific function automatically converts seconds into milliseconds, preventing common off-by-one timing errors.

questions.explanation: Temporal Workflows must be deterministic so they can be replayed from their event history after a Worker restart. A standard OS-level sleep is not recorded in history, so on replay the Workflow would behave differently. Using the SDK's Workflow sleep (e.g., `Workflow.DelayAsync`, `Workflow.sleep`) creates a durable, replayable timer event in Temporal's event history.

questions.question: In the TypeScript SDK, what is the 'racing Signals' pattern used for with Durable Timers?

choices.text: It forces a Signal to always win over a Timer so that timeouts can never occur in production.
It lets a Workflow proceed as soon as either a human sends a Signal or a set amount of time passes — whichever comes first.
It batches multiple Signals together and delivers them only after the Timer expires to reduce event overhead.
It replaces Timers entirely, using Signal counts as a substitute for tracking elapsed time.

questions.explanation: The racing Signals pattern combines a Durable Timer and a Signal using `Promise.race`. The Workflow proceeds when either the user sends a Signal (human intervention) or the timer expires — whichever happens first. This is a clean way to model real-world scenarios like 'wait up to 30 days for a user to act, then send a reminder if they haven't.'

questions.question: From an operator's perspective, what happens to a Durable Timer if the Temporal Service experiences downtime while the timer is counting down?

choices.text: The timer is permanently lost and must be restarted manually by the operator once the service is restored.
The timer resets to its full original duration when the service comes back online, extending the total wait time.
The timer fires immediately when the service restarts, regardless of whether the original duration had elapsed.
The timer's state is preserved in Temporal's durable store, so when the service and Worker come back up, it resolves correctly and the Workflow continues.

questions.explanation: This is central to Temporal's value proposition for operators: you do not need to build custom recovery logic for delayed operations. Because timer state is stored durably, an outage is transparent to the Workflow. Once the Temporal Service and a Worker are both healthy again, the timer resolves and execution continues exactly as designed — no manual intervention or data loss.

questions.question: A Temporal workflow needs to pause for 30 days waiting for a subscription renewal. The Worker process restarts multiple times during that window. What happens to the timer when the Worker comes back online after each restart?

choices.text: The timer resets to its full 30-day duration each time the Worker restarts, so the workflow may never proceed if restarts are frequent enough.
The timer is lost on restart; the workflow must re-register a new timer by re-executing the sleep call from the beginning of the workflow function.
The timer is persisted in the Temporal Service, so after each restart the Worker reconnects and the timer resolves normally when the original 30-day period elapses.
The timer pauses while the Worker is down and resumes counting only when the Worker reconnects, effectively extending the total wall-clock time by the downtime duration.

questions.explanation: Durable Timers are persisted by the Temporal Service, not by the Worker process. When the Worker restarts and reconnects, it replays the workflow history, and the timer resolves as soon as the original deadline is reached — regardless of how many times the Worker restarted. Option A is wrong because timers are never reset by Worker restarts. Option B is wrong because re-execution via replay is deterministic and the existing timer event in history is used, not a new registration. Option D is wrong because the timer counts wall-clock time from when it was originally set, not from when the Worker is actively connected.

questions.question:
In a TypeScript workflow you write the following code. What is the documented risk with this pattern?

```ts
await Promise.race([
  sleep('5s').then(() => (status = 'timed_out')),
  processOrder().then(() => (status = 'processed')),
]);
if (status === 'processed') await complete(); // takes 10 seconds
```

choices.text: Using `Promise.race` with `sleep` is non-deterministic and will cause a non-determinism error on workflow replay.
The chained `.then` on the sleep promise continues to execute after the race resolves, so `status` may be overwritten to `'timed_out'` even when `processOrder` won the race.
The `sleep` call inside `.then` chains is not supported by the Temporal TypeScript SDK and will throw at runtime.
Both `.then` callbacks run synchronously before the `await` returns, so `status` will always reflect the last callback registered, not the winner of the race.

questions.explanation: The Temporal docs explicitly call out 'Antipattern: Racing sleep.then'. When you chain `.then` onto a sleep inside `Promise.race`, the winning promise resolves the race, but the losing `.then` callback is still scheduled and will execute later. In the example, if `processOrder` finishes first, execution enters `complete()` which takes 10 seconds — during that time the 5-second sleep's `.then` fires and sets `status = 'timed_out'`, corrupting state. Option A is wrong; `sleep` is fully deterministic and replay-safe. Option C is wrong; `.then` chaining on sleep is syntactically valid. Option D is wrong; `.then` callbacks are asynchronous, not synchronous.

questions.question: Which Go SDK call causes execution to continue immediately if the provided duration is zero or negative?

choices.text: `workflow.NewTimer()` — a zero or negative duration panics with an invalid argument error at runtime.
`workflow.Sleep()` — a zero or negative duration causes the function to return immediately without scheduling a timer.
Both `workflow.NewTimer()` and `workflow.Sleep()` block for at least one workflow task before returning, regardless of the duration.
`workflow.NewTimer()` — a zero or negative duration is treated as a 1-second minimum timer by the Temporal Service.

questions.explanation: The Go SDK documentation explicitly states that a zero or negative sleep duration causes `workflow.Sleep()` to return immediately. This is a useful property when you compute a delay dynamically and want to skip sleeping if the computed duration is non-positive. `workflow.NewTimer()` is the lower-level primitive that returns a channel, and the docs do not mention the same zero-duration shortcut for it. Options A, C, and D misattribute this behavior to `NewTimer` or incorrectly claim both block.

questions.question: You are building a TypeScript workflow that should send a reminder email if a user does not complete an action within 30 days. Using `Promise.race` with a Signal and a `sleep`, which pattern correctly implements 'send email only if NO signal is received'?

choices.text: Race the signal Trigger against `sleep('30 days')`. After the race, check whether the Trigger resolved; if it did NOT resolve (sleep won), send the reminder email.
Set a Signal handler that directly calls the email function, then await `sleep('30 days')` separately so both always execute.
Await the signal Trigger alone with a timeout argument of 30 days; if it throws a TimeoutError, send the reminder email.
Race the signal Trigger against `sleep('30 days')`. After the race, check whether `userInteracted` is falsy (sleep won); if so, send the reminder email.

questions.explanation: The documentation's 'Racing Signals' example shows exactly this pattern: `const userInteracted = await Promise.race([userInteraction, sleep('30 days')])`. If the sleep wins the race, `userInteracted` is `undefined` (falsy), and you send the reminder. Option A is almost identical but incorrectly states 'if it did NOT resolve', which conflates the Trigger resolving with it winning the race — if sleep wins, the Trigger's resolved value is simply not returned by `race`, not that it never resolved. Option B always sends the email regardless of Signal receipt. Option C is not how Temporal Triggers work; they don't throw TimeoutError.

questions.question: A team running millions of concurrent Temporal workflows is worried about Worker resource consumption from all those workflows sleeping for days. How does Temporal handle the resource cost of Durable Timers?

choices.text: Each sleeping workflow holds an open goroutine or thread on the Worker for the full sleep duration, so Worker memory scales linearly with the number of concurrent timers.
Sleeping is a resource-light operation; timers do not tie up Worker processes, so millions of timers can run off a single Worker without proportional memory or CPU cost.
The Temporal Service charges each workflow a periodic heartbeat poll during sleep, consuming one task queue poll per sleeping workflow per minute.
Workers batch sleeping workflows into groups of 1,000 and schedule a single OS timer per group, reducing but not eliminating linear memory growth.

questions.explanation: All SDK documentation pages consistently state: 'Sleeping is a resource-light operation: it does not tie up the process, and you can run millions of Timers off a single Worker.' The timer state lives in the Temporal Service's persistence layer; the Worker does not hold any in-process resource for a sleeping workflow. This is one of the core value propositions of durable timers over cron jobs or database polling. Options A, C, and D all describe mechanisms that do not exist in Temporal — timers are purely server-side scheduled events.

questions.question: In the .NET SDK, what is the correct way to pause a workflow for 3 days, and how does it relate to standard .NET async primitives?

choices.text: Use `await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromDays(3))` directly; the Temporal .NET SDK automatically intercepts `Task.Delay` and makes it durable.
Use `await Workflow.DelayAsync(TimeSpan.FromDays(3))`; this is a deterministic, durable form of `Task.Delay` provided by the Temporal SDK.
Use `Thread.Sleep(259200000)` inside the workflow; the SDK serializes thread sleep calls into workflow history events.
Use `await Workflow.SleepAsync(TimeSpan.FromDays(3))`; `DelayAsync` exists but is reserved for activity-level delays only.

questions.explanation: `Workflow.DelayAsync` is the correct .NET SDK API for durable sleeping. The documentation describes it as 'a deterministic form of `Task.Delay`', meaning it has the same interface as the standard .NET primitive but is backed by the Temporal timer mechanism. Option A is wrong — you cannot use `Task.Delay` directly because it is non-deterministic and not replay-safe; the SDK does not intercept it. Option C is wrong; blocking a thread inside a workflow breaks the cooperative multitasking model and is not persisted. Option D is wrong; `SleepAsync` is not the documented API name, and `DelayAsync` is not restricted to activities.

questions.question: In the TypeScript SDK's `UpdatableTimer` pattern, how is the timer deadline changed after the workflow has already started sleeping?

choices.text: The workflow cancels the current `sleep` call and immediately starts a new `sleep` with the updated deadline by calling `sleep()` again inside a loop.
A new deadline is sent via a Signal, which updates `timer.deadline`; the internal `condition` loop in `UpdatableTimer` detects `deadlineUpdated = true` and re-evaluates the remaining duration.
The client calls `workflow.patch()` to inject a new timer event directly into the workflow history, overwriting the original deadline.
The deadline is changed by sending a Query that mutates workflow state, which is then read on the next workflow task execution.

questions.explanation: The `UpdatableTimer` implementation uses `wf.condition(() => this.deadlineUpdated, remainingMs)` inside a `while(true)` loop. When a Signal carrying a new deadline arrives, the Signal handler sets `timer.deadline` and `deadlineUpdated = true`. The `condition` unblocks early (because `deadlineUpdated` became true), the loop iterates, resets the flag, and calls `condition` again with the new remaining duration. This makes the timer effectively movable. Option A is partially right in spirit but wrong in mechanics — there is no explicit cancel/restart of `sleep`; it's all managed via `condition`. Option C is wrong; `workflow.patch` is a versioning API, not a timer-update mechanism. Option D is wrong; Queries are read-only and cannot mutate workflow state.

questions.question: In the Ruby SDK, what advantage does `Temporalio::Workflow.sleep` have over using the standard `Kernel#sleep` inside a workflow?

choices.text: `Temporalio::Workflow.sleep` runs on a background thread so it does not block other fibers, whereas `Kernel#sleep` blocks the entire Ruby process.
`Temporalio::Workflow.sleep` accepts a `summary:` keyword argument that is displayed in the Temporal UI, while `Kernel#sleep` provides no such observability metadata.
`Temporalio::Workflow.sleep` supports sub-millisecond precision durations, whereas `Kernel#sleep` rounds to the nearest second.
`Temporalio::Workflow.sleep` persists across Worker restarts, whereas `Kernel#sleep` is a volatile in-process sleep that does not survive crashes.

questions.explanation: The Ruby SDK documentation notes that technically `Kernel#sleep` works inside a workflow, but recommends `Temporalio::Workflow.sleep` specifically because 'the workflow form allows one to set a summary to view in the UI' — e.g., `summary: 'my timer'`. This is the documented, practical differentiation. Option D is a tempting distractor because durability is a key Temporal property, but the docs explicitly say `Kernel#sleep` *does* work (it is also intercepted), making durability not the distinguishing factor here. Option A is wrong; the SDK's fiber-based concurrency model handles scheduling, not separate threads. Option C is wrong; no precision difference is mentioned.

questions.question: Your team deploys a new Worker version that removes a `Workflow.DelayAsync` call that was previously sleeping for 3 days. Several long-running workflow executions are mid-sleep when the deploy happens. What will occur when these workflows replay on the new Worker code?

choices.text: Temporal skips the missing timer event during replay because timers are considered optional checkpoints, so the workflow continues from the next recorded event without error.
The workflows complete immediately because the timer call is no longer in the code, and Temporal interprets the missing await as the timer having already fired.

Replay produces a non-determinism error because the workflow history contains a TimerStarted event that the new code no longer generates, causing the replayed execution to diverge from the recorded history.
The Worker silently discards the in-progress timers and resumes execution from after the removed sleep, with a warning logged to the Worker process but no workflow failure.

questions.explanation: Temporal enforces determinism by replaying workflow history event-by-event. When a workflow execution that already recorded a `TimerStarted` event is replayed against code that no longer issues that timer, the Worker's execution diverges from the recorded history: the code tries to process the next history command but the expected timer event is missing (or the sequence is wrong). This causes a non-determinism (workflow task failure) error. This is exactly why versioning (e.g., `workflow.GetVersion` in Go, or Worker Versioning) exists — you cannot remove or reorder commands in live workflows without a versioning strategy. The timer is durable and part of history; it cannot simply be skipped.

questions.question: You have a Go workflow that uses `workflow.Sleep(ctx, 30*24*time.Hour)` to pause for 30 days. Your Temporal Service undergoes a 2-hour maintenance window on day 15. What happens to this workflow execution during and after the outage?

choices.text: The sleep timer is lost during the outage, so after recovery the workflow restarts from the beginning and re-enters the 30-day sleep from scratch.

The timer persists in the Temporal Service's durable store. After the maintenance window ends and the service recovers, the timer continues from where it left off and fires on day 30 as originally scheduled.
The workflow is marked as timed out by the cluster health monitor during the outage because no heartbeat was received from the sleeping workflow for 2 hours.
The sleep is paused for the duration of the outage and the total sleep duration is extended by 2 hours, so the timer fires on day 30 plus the 2-hour maintenance window.

questions.explanation: Durable Timers are persisted in the Temporal Service — a `TimerStarted` command is recorded in the workflow's history with an absolute fire time. The timer's deadline is wall-clock time, not a countdown measured by worker uptime. When the service comes back online after the maintenance window, the timer is evaluated against the current time. If the fire time has not yet passed, the timer simply waits the remaining duration. If it has passed, it fires immediately. The workflow does not restart, does not lose its timer, and is not timed out by the outage — sleeping workflows do not need to send heartbeats. This is the core value proposition of durable timers.

questions.question: An engineering team is building a subscription billing workflow in TypeScript that should send a reminder email if a user hasn't confirmed their account within 30 days. They write: `await Promise.race([sleep('30 days').then(() => status = 'expired'), confirmSignal.then(() => status = 'confirmed')])`. After a user confirms quickly, they observe that `status` sometimes reads as `'expired'` even though the confirmation signal arrived first. What is the root cause?

choices.text: The `confirmSignal` handler fires asynchronously in a separate microtask queue, so it always loses the race against the synchronously-chained sleep `.then` callback.

After `Promise.race` resolves with the confirmation signal, the chained `.then` on the sleep promise continues to execute on its own schedule. If the code following the race takes more than the remaining sleep duration, the `.then` side effect runs and overwrites `status`.
Temporal's deterministic runtime evaluates both branches of `Promise.race` to completion before returning control to the workflow, so the last `.then` to run wins regardless of which resolved first.
The `sleep` promise cannot be raced against a Signal trigger in Temporal because `Promise.race` is non-deterministic and is blocked by the workflow sandbox.

questions.explanation: This is the exact antipattern documented as 'Racing sleep.then'. When `Promise.race` resolves because `confirmSignal` won, the chained `.then(() => status = 'expired')` on the `sleep` promise is NOT cancelled — it remains scheduled and will execute when the 30-day timer eventually fires. If the code after `Promise.race` (e.g., `await complete()`) takes longer than the remaining sleep duration, the sleep's `.then` side effect runs and overwrites `status` with `'expired'`. The fix is to avoid chaining side-effectful `.then` callbacks onto sleeping promises in a race. Instead, check the resolved value and branch explicitly after the race, or use `wf.condition` with a timeout for cleaner cancellation semantics.

questions.question: You're running a Java-based Temporal workflow that calls `Workflow.sleep(86400)` (24 hours) inside a loop that processes daily batches. After 90 iterations, you notice the workflow's history size in the Temporal Web UI is approaching the 2 GB limit. How do timers interact with this growth pattern, and what is the correct mitigation?

choices.text: Each `Workflow.sleep()` call writes a `TimerStarted` and `TimerFired` event pair to history. After 90 iterations, 180 timer events plus activity events accumulate. The correct fix is to use Continue-As-New to reset history while preserving workflow state.
Timers do not contribute to history size because they are stored in a separate timer service and only a single pointer is recorded in the workflow's event history.
The workflow history grows because `Workflow.sleep()` creates a new workflow task after each sleep, and the fix is to batch multiple sleep calls into a single longer sleep to reduce event count.
Temporal automatically compresses timer events in long-running workflows, so timer-heavy workflows are exempt from the history size limit and the growth must be caused by activity events alone.

questions.explanation: Every `Workflow.sleep()` (and every activity, signal, etc.) appends events to the workflow's event history. Each sleep produces at minimum a `TimerStarted` and `TimerFired` event. In a loop running 90 daily batches, this adds 180+ timer events on top of all the activity and workflow task events. Temporal imposes a hard history size limit (50,000 events or ~2 GB). The standard solution for long-running, iterative workflows is Continue-As-New (`Workflow.continueAsNew()` in Java), which atomically completes the current execution and starts a fresh one with a clean history, passing the necessary state as input. This is a fundamental Temporal production pattern for workflows with unbounded iteration.

questions.question: A platform team wants to build an 'updatable deadline' feature in TypeScript: a workflow sleeping until a deadline that can be pushed forward or backward via signals. They implement it using the `UpdatableTimer` pattern with `wf.condition(() => this.deadlineUpdated, this.#deadline - Date.now())`. What happens internally when a signal extends the deadline from today+1 day to today+3 days?

choices.text: The original 1-day timer is cancelled in Temporal's timer service and replaced with a new 3-day timer, recorded as new `TimerCancelled` and `TimerStarted` events in the workflow history.

The `condition` call times out after 1 day regardless of the signal because `wf.condition` cannot extend a timer that has already been scheduled; the deadline update only takes effect on the next iteration.
Temporal extends the existing timer in place without writing new events, keeping the history size constant regardless of how many deadline updates are sent.

The signal sets `deadlineUpdated = true`, causing `wf.condition` to return `true` early (before the original timer fires). The loop then resets `deadlineUpdated` and calls `wf.condition` again with the new, longer deadline, creating a new timer command in history.

questions.explanation: The `UpdatableTimer` implementation works by looping: it calls `wf.condition` with a timeout equal to the current deadline. When a signal arrives with a new deadline, it sets `deadlineUpdated = true`, which satisfies the condition predicate and causes `wf.condition` to return `true` immediately (before the timer fires). The `while(true)` loop resets `deadlineUpdated = false`, calculates a new timeout from the updated `this.#deadline`, and issues a new `wf.condition` call — which internally schedules a new timer in Temporal. So from Temporal's perspective, a deadline extension cancels the in-flight timer and starts a new one (two history events), and deadline shortening works the same way. This has a history cost: each deadline update writes timer events, so extremely frequent updates can grow history.

questions.question: A PHP workflow yields `Workflow::timer(300)` inside an `await` call like `yield Workflow::await(fn() => $this->ready || Workflow::timer(300))`. During a code review, a senior engineer flags this as a bug. What is the documented constraint being violated?

choices.text: PHP coroutines cannot yield inside closures, so the timer invocation would throw a fiber exception at runtime rather than scheduling a durable timer.
The Temporal PHP SDK explicitly states that Timer invocations cannot be set inside `await` or `awaitWithTimeout` methods, so this usage is unsupported and will produce undefined behavior.
Mixing a boolean condition with a timer inside `await` creates two competing coroutines that Temporal cannot deterministically schedule, causing a replay mismatch on the second execution.
The `300` argument to `Workflow::timer` is interpreted as milliseconds inside `await`, not seconds, causing the timer to fire much sooner than intended.

questions.explanation: The Temporal PHP SDK documentation explicitly states: 'You cannot set a Timer invocation inside the `await` or `awaitWithTimeout` methods.' This is a hard constraint of the PHP SDK's coroutine model. Placing `Workflow::timer()` inside an `await` condition closure attempts to schedule a durable timer command from within a predicate evaluation context, which the SDK does not support. The correct pattern is to schedule the timer outside the `await` — create the timer coroutine first, then use the resulting promise/coroutine in a race or condition. This is a PHP-SDK-specific constraint; other SDKs handle this differently.

questions.question: Your operations team reports that a single Temporal Worker process is successfully managing 500,000 concurrent workflow executions, all of which are in a `Workflow.sleep()` state awaiting timers. Memory utilization on the Worker host is unexpectedly low. Which design property of Durable Timers explains this observation, and what does it mean for capacity planning?

choices.text: Sleeping workflows have their goroutines/threads suspended and swapped to disk by the OS, so the Worker consumes disk I/O rather than RAM, explaining the low in-memory footprint.

Temporal Durable Timers are managed by the Temporal Service, not the Worker. A sleeping workflow holds no thread, goroutine, or significant heap allocation on the Worker. Workers only consume resources when actively executing workflow tasks, so millions of sleeping workflows impose near-zero load on Worker hosts.
The Worker is using Temporal's built-in timer coalescing, which merges all timers with the same deadline into a single system timer, drastically reducing the per-timer memory overhead.
Low memory is observed because sleeping workflows are offloaded to a dedicated timer Worker that is auto-provisioned by the Temporal Service, separate from the application Worker process.

questions.explanation: Durable Timers are a server-side primitive. When a workflow calls `sleep()` or equivalent, it issues a `ScheduleTimer` command to the Temporal Service and completes its current workflow task. The Worker thread/goroutine/coroutine is released. The timer deadline is stored in the Temporal Service's persistence layer. The Worker holds no in-memory state for sleeping workflows — no thread, no goroutine, no heap allocation per sleeping execution. When the timer fires, the Temporal Service schedules a new workflow task, and only then does a Worker thread pick it up. This is why the documentation states 'you can run millions of Timers off a single Worker' — the Worker's resource usage is proportional to concurrently executing (not sleeping) workflow tasks, not to the total number of open executions.

questions.question: A Python workflow uses `await asyncio.sleep(10)` inside a workflow function. A developer on the team argues this is equivalent to using a regular `time.sleep(10)` and suggests switching to the standard library call for simplicity. Why is this argument incorrect, and what would actually happen if `time.sleep(10)` were used instead?

choices.text: `time.sleep(10)` would work correctly in most cases, but would fail during replay because the Temporal sandbox blocks all blocking I/O calls and throws an exception when `time.sleep` is detected.
Both calls are functionally equivalent inside a Temporal workflow because the Python SDK wraps all standard library calls to make them deterministic and durable.

`time.sleep(10)` would block the asyncio event loop thread for 10 real seconds, preventing other workflow tasks on the same worker thread from executing and producing wall-clock delays during replay that violate determinism. No durable timer command would be recorded in workflow history.
`time.sleep(10)` creates a non-durable timer that is lost on Worker restart, but the sleep duration is short enough that this is acceptable for production use cases under 60 seconds.

questions.explanation: In a Temporal Python workflow, `asyncio.sleep()` is intercepted by the SDK and translated into a durable `ScheduleTimer` command recorded in workflow history — it suspends the coroutine without blocking the event loop and survives Worker restarts. In contrast, `time.sleep(10)` is a synchronous, blocking call that occupies the asyncio event loop thread for 10 real seconds. This has three serious consequences: (1) it blocks all other coroutines on that thread from running, degrading Worker throughput; (2) it introduces real wall-clock delay during deterministic replay, potentially causing the replayed execution to diverge from recorded history timing; and (3) it writes no timer event to the workflow history, meaning if the Worker crashes during the sleep, the workflow restarts and re-runs the sleep from scratch (the sleep is NOT durable). Using non-SDK sleep calls inside workflows is a common and dangerous antipattern.
